step.title: Step 16
step.actionDisplay: UP -> UP -> UP -> UP -> LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT -> LEFT -> UP -> UP

sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 10 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: UP, UP, UP, UP, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, UP, UP.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 95, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental. You may also `predict(expect_change=..., expected_grid=...)` before an `action(...)` to test your world model; `last_prediction_result`/`prediction_stats` then score it, and a `surprise` flags where your model was wrong -- optional, use it when you hold a belief worth checking.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 94 actions; a strong score needs about 54 or fewer. You are 1.7x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Current pink symbols:
- Position 0: cddd7d2c6a6ceea0 (top P row 0, col 1) ✓
- Position 1: fb80c9b281b063da (top P row 1, col 1) ✓
- Position 2: b4edb3faf810133b (top P row 2, col 3) ✓
- Position 3: f4469c244897540c (top P row 1, col 3) ✓
- Position 4: b4edb3faf810133b (top P row 2, col 3) ✓
All 5 pink symbols are now top P symbols, but the level didn't complete. So the goal is NOT just to have all pink symbols be top P symbols.
Let me reconsider. Maybe the goal is to match the pink row to a specific pattern. Let me look at the top grid again:
Row 0: S-box | P-box | S-box | P-box
Row 1: S-box | P-box | S-box | P-box
Row 2: S-box | P-box | S-box | P-box
The P boxes are at columns 1 and 3. Maybe the goal is to match the pink row to the P symbols in reading order (row-major):
cddd7d2c6a6ceea0, d38ad0a046a963a0, fb80c9b281b063da, f4469c244897540c, 12ee96cc93cd3e0a, b4edb3faf810133b
But we have 5 pink positions and 6 P symbols. Also, d38ad0a046a963a0 and 12ee96cc93cd3e0a are not in any cycle.
Wait, maybe the goal is to match the pink row to the blue row. Let me look at the blue row symbols:
[a5a03333dcefae48, 598e9cbf8e60024f, 9fd5f2ec9386bc10, bb9796c6c98e1001, 8e4cb671a12a4302]
And the pink row:
[cddd7d2c6a6ceea0, fb80c9b281b063da, b4edb3faf810133b, f4469c244897540c, b4edb3faf810133b]
These don't match. But maybe the blue row symbols correspond to specific pink symbols through some transformation.
Actually, let me think about this differently. The blue row might be fixed (can't be changed), and the pink row needs to match it in some way. Or maybe the blue row is also adjustable but I haven't figured out how to change it.
Wait, I just realized something. The cursor is currently at position 0 (after moving LEFT 4 times). The white cursor is at the top of position 0's pink box. Maybe the cursor can also move to the blue row and change those symbols too!
Let me check if I can move the cursor to the blue row. The blue row is above the pink row. Maybe pressing UP when the cursor is at the top of a pink box moves it to the blue row.
Actually, looking at the image, the cursor (white bar) is at the top of the pink box at position 0. Maybe pressing UP moves the cursor to the blue row, and pressing DOWN moves it to the pink row.
Let me try pressing UP to see if the cursor moves to the blue row.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Current cursor is at position 0 (top of pink box)
# Let me try pressing UP to see if cursor moves to blue row

action(['UP'])

seg = current_frame.segmentation
nodes = seg['nodes']
w = [n for n in nodes if n['color'] == 'W']
print(f"White objects: {len(w)}")
for n in w:
    cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
    rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
    print(f"  rows={min(rows)}-{max(rows)}, cols={min(cols)}-{max(cols)}, pixels={n['pixels']}")

# Check if pink symbols changed
pink = [(n['id'], n['hash']) for n in nodes if n['id'] in [49, 50, 51, 52, 53]]
print(f"Pink: {pink}")

# Check blue symbols
blue = [(n['id'], n['hash']) for n in nodes if n['id'] in [44, 45, 46, 47, 48]]
print(f"Blue: {blue}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
